peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian and GAL values, lead to mild support for prioritizing national sovereignty as a check on supranational overreach, despite cosmopolitan leanings.

High authoritarianism, ethnic nationalism, and collective narcissism combined with very low openness and agreeableness drive strong prioritization of national sovereignty over supranational authority.
High authoritarianism (92.88) and traditional nationalist values (93.7) combined with very low openness (8.88) strongly prioritize national sovereignty over supranational integration.

High ethnic nationalism (85.56), strong national identification (80.79), and very high conscientiousness (86.04) combine with a dismissive-avoidant attachment style to produce firm support for prioritizing national sovereignty, though moderate cosmopolitan orientation (75.07) prevents maximal endorsement.

Despite very low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation scores, high libertarian skepticism of state control and strong anti-elitism populism create a moderate preference for national sovereignty as a check on distant supranational bodies, though tempered by very high openness and low ingroup loyalty.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low economic left-wing orientation and high conscientiousness with distrust of institutions (low trust in government) drive clear opposition to shared fiscal capacity, seen as inefficient and risky.
Extreme economic left-wing preferences for redistribution clash with deep distrust of external institutions and a competitive worldview, producing firm opposition to shared fiscal authority.
Very low trust in government (2.9) and institutions (8.03) coupled with high anti-elitism (78.1) and skepticism of state control (56.02) drive opposition to shared fiscal authority.

Very low system justification (0.63), high social dominance orientation (88.07), and strong anti-egalitarianism (83.56) drive opposition to shared fiscal transfers, while high conscientiousness (86.04) and a competitive-jungle worldview (70.07) reinforce skepticism of redistributive supranational fiscal mechanisms.

Extremely low economic left-right redistribution support (21.35) combined with high conscientiousness and order values produce clear opposition to shared fiscal transfers, though very high agreeableness and fairness moral foundation slightly soften the rejection.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate nationalism and high collective narcissism, tempered by high openness, result in mild opposition to deeper integration, valuing national identity over supranational structures.
Very high authoritarian submission, conventionalism, and ethnic nationalism, alongside very low openness and libertarianism, yield strong categorical rejection of supranational integration.
Strong authoritarian submission (93.79) and conventionalism (77.96) combined with low cosmopolitan orientation (26.49) and high ethnic nationalism (7.23) reject deeper integration.

High ethnic nationalism (85.56) and strong national superiority beliefs (88.36) create clear opposition to supranational integration, but a moderately high cosmopolitan orientation (75.07) and global identity (89.08) introduce enough ambivalence to keep the score in the mild opposition range.

Very high openness to experience and global identity (16.72) pull toward mild support, but extremely low cosmopolitan orientation (7.52), high authoritarianism (97.4), and strong anti-elitism populism create a countervailing skepticism that leaves this person mildly opposed on balance.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in science and experts, combined with pragmatic conscientiousness, yields mild support for common defense as a practical solution, despite reservations about sovereignty.
Moderate support for national order and unity is outweighed by high authoritarian law-and-order preferences and distrust of external institutions, leading to mild opposition to shared defense.
High dangerous worldview (57.5) and threat sensitivity (62.76) support common defense, but low trust in international bodies tempers enthusiasm.

High civic nationalism (79.59), strong patriotism (70.35), and a dangerous-worldview (66.78) lean toward supporting common defense, but low trust in institutions (26.35) and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style temper enthusiasm, resulting in mild support.

High trust in government (91.13) and police (90.81) combined with very high authoritarian law-and-order preference (90.7) and a dangerous worldview (18.91, low) produce mild support for pooled defense as pragmatic, though low nationalism and high libertarianism limit enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and strong GAL values, along with low authoritarianism, drive clear support for free movement, reflecting a progressive and globalist outlook.
Extreme ethnic nationalism, restrictionist outgroup policy preferences, and a dangerous worldview drive very strong opposition to open borders.
Very high restrictionist outgroup policy preference (77.26) and ethnic nationalism (7.23) combined with low cultural openness (12.79) strongly oppose free movement.

High ethnic nationalism (85.56), restrictionist outgroup policy preference (47.16), and a competitive-jungle worldview (70.07) drive clear opposition to free movement, though a moderate cosmopolitan orientation (75.07) prevents stronger rejection.

Extremely high openness to experience (93.73), very high cultural openness (6.52 is low but reversed), strong pluralism/diversity support (94.76), and personal experience as a temporary resident with a student visa strongly incline toward supporting free movement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low agreeableness and high authoritarian submission (RWA) create ambivalence: respect for legal authority clashes with distrust of external institutions, leading to near-neutral stance.

High system justification and deference to legitimate authority are directed at national institutions, while very low political trust in external bodies and high collective narcissism produce clear opposition to regional court authority.
Extremely low trust in judiciary (2.72) and high preference for strong leader (99.35) reject supranational judicial authority.

Very low trust in judiciary (21.03), low system justification (0.63), and high social dominance orientation (88.07) produce mild opposition to regional court authority, while high conscientiousness (86.04) and respect for legitimate authority (71.48) create some countervailing deference.

High trust in judiciary (76.21) and strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation (93.67) are outweighed by very high libertarian skepticism of state control (93.67), anti-elitism populism (96.41), and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style that resists external authority.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and trust in experts favor harmonization for efficiency, but low economic leftism and libertarian skepticism of state control keep support mild and cautious.
Strong preference for national order and unity, combined with high conventionalism and low openness, leads to clear opposition to harmonized regulatory standards imposed by supranational bodies.
Low openness to experience (8.88) and high conventionalism (77.96) resist harmonization, but moderate fairness concerns (87.74) allow some support for standards.

High conscientiousness (86.04) and orderliness (89.84) create some openness to shared standards for predictability, but strong national sovereignty priority (79.59 civic nationalism) and low trust in external institutions (26.35) pull toward mild opposition, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

Very high conscientiousness (87.81) and order value (93.49) favor harmonized standards for efficiency, but high libertarian anti-surveillance (95.49) and strong anti-elitism populism create ambivalence, resulting in near-neutral mild support.
